name: Valverde del Camino, Calañas, Valdelamusa, El Cerro de Andévelo – circular route
name_original: Valverde del Camino, Calañas, Valdelamusa, El Cerro de Andévelo – ruta circular
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Valverde Del Camino traverse a landscape characterized by rolling hills, olive groves, and cork oak forests. The region is situated within the Sierra de Aracena and Picos de Aroche Natural Park, offering varied topography for cyclists. It lies between the valleys of the Tinto and Odiel rivers, providing diverse terrain. The area features a network of routes with varying lengths, elevation gains, and difficulty levels, including sections of Vías Verdes (Greenways) that offer flatter terrain. Best road cycling routes around Valverde Del Camino The most popular road cycling route is Valverde del Camino, Calañas, and Zalamea la Real Loop, a difficult 38.6 miles (62.1 km) trail that takes 3 hours 6 minutes to complete. This route features significant elevation gain through the region's varied terrain. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Niebla – Niebla Castle loop from Estación de Servicio Deoil Los Pinos, a moderate 51.3 miles (82.5 km) path. This route passes historical attractions such as Niebla Castle, offering cultural points of interest. Local road cyclists also love the Beas loop from Valverde del Camino, a 33.2 miles (53.4 km) trail leading through rolling hills and countryside, often completed in about 2 hours 26 minutes. Road cycling around Valverde Del Camino is defined by rolling hills, cork oak forests, and river valleys. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate loops to more challenging routes with significant climbs. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 200 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Valverde Del Camino's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around Valverde Del Camino?
A: There are &lt;em&gt;18&lt;/em&gt; dedicated road cycling routes around Valverde Del Camino listed on komoot. These routes offer a variety of distances and difficulty levels to suit different preferences.

Q: What is the typical terrain like for road cycling in Valverde Del Camino?
A: The region is characterized by rolling hills, olive groves, and cork oak forests, situated within the Sierra de Aracena and Picos de Aroche Natural Park. You'll encounter varied topography, including sections between the Tinto and Odiel river valleys. Expect a mix of ascents and descents, with some routes featuring significant elevation gain.

Q: What are the best times of year for road cycling in Valverde Del Camino?
A: Spring and autumn are ideal for road cycling in Valverde Del Camino. The climate features hot, dry summers and mild, wet winters, making the shoulder seasons perfect for enjoying the routes with more comfortable temperatures.

Q: Are there routes suitable for beginners or those looking for easier rides?
A: Yes, there are several moderate routes available. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20121915" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Beas loop from Valverde del Camino&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 33.2-mile (53.4 km) trail with manageable rolling hills. Additionally, the Vías Verdes (Greenways) in the area, like the Vía Verde Molino de Acha, offer generally flatter terrain suitable for various fitness levels.

Q: Are there challenging routes for experienced road cyclists?
A: Absolutely. For experienced cyclists seeking a challenge, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e991320335/valverde-del-camino-calanas-and-zalamea-la-real-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valverde del Camino, Calañas, and Zalamea la Real Loop&lt;/a&gt; offer significant elevation gain over 38.6 miles (62.1 km). Another demanding option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e991622266/valverde-del-camino-calanas-valdelamusa-el-cerro-de-andevelo-circular-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valverde del Camino, Calañas, Valdelamusa, El Cerro de Andévelo – circular route&lt;/a&gt;, which covers nearly 74 miles (119 km) with over 1,400 meters of ascent.

Q: What historical or cultural landmarks can I see while road cycling?
A: Many routes offer glimpses of the region's rich history. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24522925" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Niebla – Niebla Castle loop from Estación de Servicio Deoil Los Pinos&lt;/a&gt; passes by the historic Niebla Castle and the town of Niebla. You can also find ancient sites like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5765310" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Dolmens of Los Gabrieles&lt;/a&gt;, dating back to the Bronze Age, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5301118" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Roman bridge over the Odiel River&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there natural features or scenic viewpoints along the routes?
A: Yes, the region is rich in natural beauty. You can cycle through picturesque cork oak forests, olive groves, and river valleys. Highlights include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5301124" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Calabazar Reservoir&lt;/a&gt; and the scenic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20122632" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Berrocal Viewpoint – Río Tinto loop from Valverde del Camino&lt;/a&gt;, which offers expansive views.

Q: Are there any Vías Verdes (Greenways) suitable for road cycling in the area?
A: Yes, the area features Vías Verdes, which are old railway lines converted into cycling paths. The Vía Verde Molino de Acha is a notable example, stretching 33.2 km through countryside, pine forests, and cork oak meadows. The Riotinto greenway, part of Eurovelo 1, also follows an ancient mining railway for 36 km, showcasing the region's mining heritage.

Q: What kind of elevation gain can I expect on road cycling routes?
A: Elevation gains vary significantly. Moderate routes might have around 450 meters (e.g., &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20121915" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Beas loop from Valverde del Camino&lt;/a&gt;), while more difficult routes can feature over 900 meters (e.g., &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e991320335/valverde-del-camino-calanas-and-zalamea-la-real-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valverde del Camino, Calañas, and Zalamea la Real Loop&lt;/a&gt;) or even over 1,400 meters of ascent on longer, challenging loops.

Q: Are there family-friendly road cycling options?
A: While many road cycling routes involve rolling hills, the Vías Verdes (Greenways) are generally known for their relatively flat terrain, making them more accessible for families or those seeking a gentler ride. These paths often avoid traffic and provide a pleasant cycling experience through nature.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes?
A: Yes, many of the road cycling routes around Valverde Del Camino are designed as loops. Examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e991320335/valverde-del-camino-calanas-and-zalamea-la-real-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valverde del Camino, Calañas, and Zalamea la Real Loop&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e991622266/valverde-del-camino-calanas-valdelamusa-el-cerro-de-andevelo-circular-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valverde del Camino, Calañas, Valdelamusa, El Cerro de Andévelo – circular route&lt;/a&gt;, allowing you to start and finish in the same location.

Q: What are some longer distance road cycling routes available?
A: For longer rides, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e991622266/valverde-del-camino-calanas-valdelamusa-el-cerro-de-andevelo-circular-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valverde del Camino, Calañas, Valdelamusa, El Cerro de Andévelo – circular route&lt;/a&gt;, which spans nearly 74 miles (119 km). Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20122632" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Berrocal Viewpoint – Río Tinto loop from Valverde del Camino&lt;/a&gt;, covering approximately 64.7 miles (104 km).
tips: Located in front of the Hermitage of the Virgin of Spain, of which there is an oral and written tradition of being built next to a Roman necropolis. It says that it was built after the appearance of the Virgin of Coronada in the year 1520. The Virgin was found by Andúvalo from Calañés in the year 720, who, during the Muslim invasion, hid her under a rock where she made her appearance in the year 1520 to a shepherd from Valverde named Pedro Márquez. This rock is found in the Sanctuary under the High Altar.

Calañas, in the province of Huelva, is a town with a rich mining history, a privileged natural environment, and a deeply rooted culture.
Ancient Origins: It was a mining enclave from the Tartessian and Roman periods, with finds at "El Morante" including amphorae, tombstones, and coins.
Middle Ages: Under Muslim, then Christian, rule, it passed to the Guzmán family. Livestock farming was the main activity.
19th Century: Mining revived (pyrite, copper, iron, manganese), and the railway arrived in 1886. Calañas reached 12,000 inhabitants in 1910.
20th Century: The closure of mines and subsistence farming led to depopulation. Today, it seeks to revitalize itself through tourism and culture.
The flooded Valdelamusa quarry, also known as the Corta de los Confesionarios, is one of the most striking elements of the mining heritage of this district of Cortegana, in the province of Huelva.

The name of the town is due to the fact that it is located on the slope of a hill and the main monument of the population is the Church of Santa María de Gracia. The Pilgrimage of San Benito Abad, declared National Tourist Interest of Andalusia, is a sample of the cultural richness of this place, which also houses the Hermitage of San Benito Abad (where the image of the saint and patron saint of El Cerro de Andévalo is located) , a place much visited by tourists.
Church from the 17th century, with the carving of the Virgen del Reposo, patron saint of Valverde since 1672.
